item.title: Critical Appraisal
item.description:
Critical appraisal is a structured method for judging the trustworthiness, relevance, and applicability of evidence before using it in clinical reasoning or care decisions.

The practice originates in evidence-based medicine (EBM) and centers on a systematic set of questions applied to any piece of evidence — whether a randomized controlled trial, an observational study, a clinical guideline, or a FOAM blog post:

  1. Is the source trustworthy? Who produced this content? What is their expertise? Do they cite primary evidence? Are conflicts of interest disclosed?
  2. Is the evidence valid? What study design was used? Was the methodology sound? Are the results statistically and clinically significant? Could bias explain the findings?
  3. Is the evidence relevant? Does this apply to my patient population? Were the study participants similar to the patients I treat? Does the clinical context match?
  4. Is the evidence applicable? Can I implement these findings in my practice setting? Do I have the resources, skills, and institutional support? Does this conflict with existing local protocols?

In the context of FOAM consumption, critical appraisal takes on additional dimensions. FOAM content is typically not peer-reviewed, may not cite primary sources, and is shaped by the same platform incentives (engagement, novelty, shareability) that distort information in other domains. Red flags for FOAM content include:

item.title: Free Open-Access Medical Education (FOAMed)
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medical Education (FOAMed) refers to the educational dimension of FOAM: openly accessible clinical teaching, critique, and peer discussion produced and consumed outside traditional institutional channels.

Where FOAM describes the broader movement of open medical knowledge-sharing, FOAMed focuses specifically on content designed to teach. A blog post summarizing the evidence on a clinical question is FOAMed. A podcast walking through the approach to a difficult airway is FOAMed. A social media thread critiquing a newly published trial’s methodology is FOAMed. The distinction matters because not all FOAM is educational — some is advocacy, some is community-building, some is professional networking — and the standards for evaluating educational content differ from those for evaluating opinion or commentary.

item.title: Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM)
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) is the open sharing of medical education resources across web-native formats — blog posts, podcasts, videos, infographics, social media threads, and discussion forums — with emphasis on rapid dissemination and practical utility.

The term was coined by emergency physician Mike Cadogan in 2012, drawing on the culture of open knowledge-sharing that had been developing in emergency medicine and critical care communities since the mid-2000s. FOAM is not a platform or an organization but a movement: anyone who produces or shares openly accessible medical education content participates in FOAM.

item.title: Pretest Probability
item.description:
Pretest probability is the estimated likelihood of a condition being present before a diagnostic test result is known. It is one of the most important concepts in clinical reasoning — and one of the most frequently violated in informal medical education.

The concept comes from Bayesian reasoning. A test result does not tell you whether a patient has a disease. It tells you how much to update your estimate of the probability that the patient has the disease, given what you already knew before the test. A positive troponin in a patient with crushing chest pain, diaphoresis, and ST-elevation on ECG means something very different from a positive troponin in a young, healthy patient with atypical symptoms and a normal ECG. The test is the same; the pretest probability is different; the clinical meaning is different.
